item.title: Under the sea...
item.content:encoded: Not really liked till the underwater society, then very very interesting! Vell written all over. +1

item.title: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded: The images that were used in this article are non-compliant with the SCP wiki's license (CC BY-SA 3.0) as the first image comes from here with no indication it has been released under Creative Commons, and no source found indicates the second image has been released under Creative Commons. Thus, the images have been removed.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The oldest living -2 is from a vessel not on the list of -1s.

I can't tell if this means that not all sunken ships enter the ghost rotation, or if the anonymous steamship -1-5 should be noted as possibly/probably the vessel D&C.
It having sunk in 1957 would fit the chronology of -1-5 at least.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Gordon Lightfoot would be proud. +1

item.title: Edmund Fitzgerald
item.content:encoded: Right when I heard that I started playing the song in my head.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I was looking up pictures of the edmund fitzgerald for my Tech class, and when I came across the 2nd picture i jumped thinking it was an actual dead crewmember

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I have actually seen the ghost of the Big Fitz. I hired out on an ore-boat as a deckhand last year after I graduated to earn a little extra money. The name was the Arthur M. Anderson. One day last November, there was a thick fog. We were close to where the Fitz went down. A ship appeared off our starboard. WE could make out the silhouette of another freighter. It had a very familiar shape. Repeated hails did nothing, and it vanished into the fog after a few minutes. My great-uncle twice removed, was a deckhand on the Fitz. When I got home, I looked through an old family photo album, and sure enough, there was a picture of him in front of the Fitz a few years before she sank. I swear to the Lord and all that is Holy that I am telling the truth.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The legend lives on from the Chippewa on down
of the big lake they call "Gitche Gumee."
"Superior," they said, "never gives up her dead
when the gales of November come early!"

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I actually just noticed that. It was an accident.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Oi, cut the sig.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: You never know. They may have been discovered out of order and they just decided to put them in chronological order.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: this implies a bit of a social structure in order for disapproval to be effective in preventing cannibalism, so I am wondering, especially since they retain memory of the former self…do they try to make the best out of what is near to construct a "home" and bring about some shadow of a former life? Besides eat and hunt, do they just…lurk?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is a really nitpicky criticism, but: the numbering of the known instances of 1174-1-X implies that they were discovered and catalogued in precisely the same order that they sank. That strikes me as improbable - you'd expect at least some of them to be numbered out of sequence due to the order in which they were first documented.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: A shame the Robert Mackenzie wasn't real. Damn you, due South…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I really, really, really like this. The fact that it doesn't kill, maim or crazify people, just changes them fundamentally into creatures of the deep, unable to ever leave… that really makes it scary for me. Reminds me of the myths about the souls of drowned sailors becoming dolphins, or merpeople, or things like that. Cool and scary as hell.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Neat-o

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Ehhh, all I could hear was the song. That second picture is awful, too. He looks like a zombie going through his teenage angry-at-the-world phase. Give him a little white makeup and he'd be Heath Ledger's Joker.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Odd, would have sworn there'd been more. It's better without that one regardless, and with a fresh set of eyes I'm no longer sure why I donwnvoted, so vote canceled.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This veers very close to underwater zombies, but the fact that they still retain their minds, actually discourage eating people, and age as usual redeems it for me. Upvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Nicely done.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Generally, I like it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I love you.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I found it by trawling Google Images. I think it's originally a still frame from a B-grade zombie movie.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
?

There's only one redaction, near the very end. I suppose I could change it if it's that big a problem.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
A SCP of the Edmund Fitzgerald?
Have a good ole Michigander upvote.
Also, that second picture is damn creepy. Where did you find it?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The community of corpses is an interesting touch, but the rest of the concept is just stock ghost ship. The redactions don't help much either.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I love this concept, I love the execution, and it's close enough to home that it chills me out.
Not to mention, I don't feel like being enslaved to the depths of Lake Superior for all time.

Upvoted.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Hmm…well written, but I'm neutral on the idea. I'll give it another look tomorrow, see if anything about it catches my eye then.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I liked this SCP since i first heard the idea. For a second i though this was going to be in the bermuda triangle, and i'm glad you didn't go there

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I tend to dislike SCPs based on real events, especially those that kill lots of people (one of which was someone I knew when I was younger).

It's well written but I can't get over the concept, sorry.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
And now, an SCP that wormed its way into my head after I listened to "The Wreck of the Edmund Fitzgerald" too many times.

Dedicated to the men who perished on that vessel and to all men and women who have died sailing the Great Lakes.
